book.name: Proverbs
book.author: Primarily Solomon (chapters 1-29), with contributions from Agur son of Jakeh (ch. 30) and King Lemuel's mother (ch. 31). Hezekiah's scribes compiled additional Solomonic proverbs (25-29).
book.dateWritten: c. 970-700 BC (Solomon's proverbs composed c. 970-930 BC; compiled over subsequent centuries)
book.writtenFrom: Jerusalem and the royal court of Israel
book.writtenTo: Young men being trained for leadership, and by extension all who seek wisdom for practical living

explainer.overview: Proverbs is the Bible's primary handbook for practical wisdom. It teaches how to live skillfully -- making good decisions in relationships, work, speech, finances, and character. The book opens with extended speeches from a father to a son (chapters 1-9), personifying Wisdom as a woman calling out in the streets and Folly as a seductress luring the naive. Chapters 10-29 are collections of short, pithy sayings covering every aspect of daily life. Chapter 30 contains the mysterious words of Agur, and chapter 31 includes the famous poem about the 'virtuous woman.' The book's foundation is clear: 'The fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge' (1:7).
explainer.historicalContext: Solomon composed 3,000 proverbs (1 Kings 4:32), and this book preserves a curated selection. Proverbs belong to the international 'wisdom literature' tradition -- similar collections existed in Egypt (Instruction of Amenemope), Mesopotamia, and other ancient cultures. Israel's distinctive contribution was grounding wisdom in the fear of Yahweh rather than in human observation alone. The book was compiled over centuries, with Hezekiah's scribes (c. 700 BC) adding a final collection of Solomonic sayings.
explainer.whyItMatters: Proverbs offers time-tested wisdom for navigating the complexities of human life. Its observations about laziness, speech, anger, relationships, and money are as accurate today as when they were written. The book insists that there is a moral order to the universe -- choices have consequences, character determines destiny, and wisdom is the most valuable possession one can acquire. While it acknowledges exceptions (as Ecclesiastes and Job explore), its general principles provide a reliable framework for decision-making.
keyThemes: The fear of the LORD as wisdom's foundation

people.name: Lady Wisdom (personified)
people.description: Wisdom personified as a noble woman who calls out publicly, inviting all to learn. She was present at creation (ch. 8), offers life to those who find her, and stands in contrast to Lady Folly. Many Christians see her as prefiguring Christ.

people.name: Lady Folly (personified)

explainer.summary: The book's introduction establishes its purpose: gaining wisdom, instruction, and understanding. A father urges his son to heed parental teaching and resist the enticement of sinners who promise easy wealth through violence. Lady Wisdom cries out in the streets, warning that those who reject her counsel will face calamity.
themes: The fear of the LORD as wisdom's foundation
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 1 is the gateway to the entire book. Verse 7 ('The fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge') is the book's thesis statement. The personification of Wisdom as a woman calling publicly introduces a major motif of chapters 1-9.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A comparison, parable, or pithy saying
wordStudies.meaning: A comparison, parable, or pithy saying. The root means 'to be like' -- proverbs teach by comparison and analogy, making abstract truth concrete.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Reverent awe before God -- not terror but deep res
wordStudies.meaning: Reverent awe before God -- not terror but deep respect that shapes all behavior. This is the foundation of the entire wisdom tradition.
Experiential knowledge gained through relationship, not just intellectual data. Knowing God personally is the beginning of all true understanding.

verses.text: The proverbs of Solomon, the son of David, king of Israel:
verses.explainer: A proverb about leadership and authority. Many proverbs were composed for training future leaders, making governance and justice frequent themes.

verses.text:
to know wisdom and instruction;
to discern the words of understanding;
verses.explainer: A proverb about speech -- one of Proverbs' most frequent topics. Words have real power to build up or tear down, and controlling the tongue is presented as one of wisdom's hardest and most important disciplines.

verses.text:
to receive instruction in wise dealing,
in righteousness, justice, and equity;
verses.explainer: A proverb about discipline and correction. Accepting reproof is a hallmark of wisdom; rejecting it is a hallmark of folly. Growth requires the humility to be corrected.

verses.text:
to give prudence to the simple,

to understand a proverb, and parables,

verses.text:
The fear of Yahweh is the beginning of knowledge;
but the foolish despise wisdom and instruction.
verses.explainer: The book's thesis statement and foundation: all genuine knowledge begins with reverent awe before God. Without this starting point, information accumulates but wisdom never forms.

thenVsNow.then: The 'fear of the LORD' is not the cringing dread of a tyrant but the awed reverence before a holy and loving God. In every era, genuine wisdom begins with recognizing God's reality and authority.

explainer.summary: A father promises that seeking wisdom like hidden treasure leads to understanding the fear of the LORD. Wisdom protects against the way of evil men and the seductive adulteress. The upright will inhabit the land while the wicked are cut off.

explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 2 presents wisdom as something that must be actively pursued -- it does not come passively. The rewards (protection from evil and seduction) motivate the search.

wordStudies.meaning: Buried treasure. Wisdom must be sought with the same intensity as someone digging for buried gold -- with effort, persistence, and single-minded focus.

explainer.summary: Trust in the LORD with all your heart and lean not on your own understanding. Honor God with your wealth. Accept His discipline. Wisdom is more precious than rubies -- she offers long life, riches, and peace. Do not withhold good from those who deserve it. Do not envy the violent.
themes: The value of wisdom above riches
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 3 contains some of the most quoted verses in all of Proverbs (3:5-6, 3:9-10). It presents the positive rewards of wisdom -- prosperity, peace, and God's favor -- while warning against self-reliance.

wordStudies.meaning: To lean on, rely upon, feel secure in. It implies casting your full weight on something -- total dependence, not partial reliance.

wordStudies.meaning: From 'yada' -- to know intimately, to recognize and involve. 'Acknowledge Him in all your ways' means involving God in every decision, not just religious ones.

explainer.summary: A father watches from his window as a naive young man is seduced by an adulteress. She catches him, kisses him, and lures him to her perfumed bed while her husband is away. He follows her like an ox to slaughter. Many have been her victims.

I looked out through my lattice.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
I saw among the simple ones.
I discerned among the youths a young man void of understanding,

verses.text:
passing through the street near her corner,
he went the way to her house,
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

in the twilight, in the evening of the day,
in the middle of the night and in the darkness.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

and with crafty intent.
verses.explainer: A proverb about marriage and domestic life. Proverbs recognizes that the quality of one's closest relationship profoundly shapes the quality of one's entire life.

verses.explainer: A proverb about leadership and authority. Many proverbs were composed for training future leaders, making governance and justice frequent themes.

verses.text:
So she caught him, and kissed him.
With an impudent face she said to him:
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
“Sacrifices of peace offerings are with me.
Today I have paid my vows.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
Therefore I came out to meet you,
to diligently seek your face,
and I have found you.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
I have spread my couch with carpets of tapestry,

verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text: I have perfumed my bed with myrrh, aloes, and cinnamon.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

For my husband isn’t at home.
He has gone on a long journey.
verses.explainer: A proverb about marriage and domestic life. Proverbs recognizes that the quality of one's closest relationship profoundly shapes the quality of one's entire life.

He has taken a bag of money with him.

verses.text:
With persuasive words, she led him astray.
With the flattering of her lips, she seduced him.
verses.explainer: A proverb about speech -- one of Proverbs' most frequent topics. Words have real power to build up or tear down, and controlling the tongue is presented as one of wisdom's hardest and most important disciplines.

verses.text:
He followed her immediately,

verses.explainer: An observation about fools -- those who reject wisdom and pursue destructive patterns. Proverbs uses 'fool' not for intellectual deficiency but for moral and spiritual stubbornness.

verses.text:
Until an arrow strikes through his liver,

verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:

Pay attention to the words of my mouth.
verses.explainer: A proverb about speech -- one of Proverbs' most frequent topics. Words have real power to build up or tear down, and controlling the tongue is presented as one of wisdom's hardest and most important disciplines.

verses.text:
Don’t let your heart turn to her ways.
Don’t go astray in her paths,
verses.explainer: A proverb about the heart -- the center of thought, will, and decision-making in Hebrew understanding. What happens internally determines everything that happens externally.

verses.text:
for she has thrown down many wounded.
Yes, all her slain are a mighty army.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

explainer.summary: Lady Wisdom speaks at length: she calls from the heights, the crossroads, and the city gates. She offers truth, righteousness, and wealth to all who listen. In the book's most exalted passage, she declares she was with God before creation -- 'the first of his works, before the world began.' Kings rule by her counsel.
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 8 is the theological peak of Proverbs. Wisdom's pre-existence at creation (8:22-31) became one of the most important passages for early Christian theology, as the church saw Christ as the fulfillment of personified Wisdom.

wordStudies.meaning: Acquired, created, or brought forth. The exact meaning in 8:22 is debated: was Wisdom 'created' by God or 'possessed' by God from eternity? This was a major theological controversy in early Christianity.
Master craftsman or architect. Wisdom was beside God as a 'master craftsman' during creation -- actively involved in forming the world.
Playful delight, pleasure. Wisdom 'delighted' in God's creation and in humanity. This word suggests joyful, playful involvement rather than detached observation.

verses.text:
Doesn’t wisdom cry out?
Doesn’t understanding raise her voice?
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
On the top of high places by the way,
where the paths meet, she stands.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
“To you men, I call!
I send my voice to the sons of mankind.
verses.explainer: A proverb about family relationships and parental instruction. Proverbs sees the family as the primary school of wisdom, where character is formed through teaching, discipline, and example.

verses.text:
You simple, understand prudence.
You fools, be of an understanding heart.
verses.explainer: An observation about fools -- those who reject wisdom and pursue destructive patterns. Proverbs uses 'fool' not for intellectual deficiency but for moral and spiritual stubbornness.

verses.text:

verses.text:

verses.explainer: A proverb about speech -- one of Proverbs' most frequent topics. Words have real power to build up or tear down, and controlling the tongue is presented as one of wisdom's hardest and most important disciplines.

verses.text:
All the words of my mouth are in righteousness.

verses.explainer: A proverb about speech -- one of Proverbs' most frequent topics. Words have real power to build up or tear down, and controlling the tongue is presented as one of wisdom's hardest and most important disciplines.

They are all plain to him who understands,

verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.explainer: A proverb about wealth and poverty. Proverbs is realistic about money: it acknowledges its benefits while warning against trusting it, hoarding it, or gaining it dishonestly.

verses.text:

verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:

verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

The fear of Yahweh is to hate evil.
I hate pride, arrogance, the evil way, and the perverse mouth.
verses.explainer: A proverb grounding practical wisdom in theology -- connecting daily behavior to God's character, will, or sovereign action. In Proverbs, the spiritual and the practical are never separated.

verses.text:
Counsel and sound knowledge are mine.
I have understanding and power.
verses.explainer: A proverb about planning and counsel. Wise decisions require input from multiple advisors -- no one person sees every angle of a situation.

verses.text:

and princes decree justice.
verses.explainer: A proverb about leadership and authority. Many proverbs were composed for training future leaders, making governance and justice frequent themes.

verses.text:
By me princes rule;
nobles, and all the righteous rulers of the earth.
verses.explainer: A proverb about leadership and authority. Many proverbs were composed for training future leaders, making governance and justice frequent themes.

verses.text:
I love those who love me.

verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
With me are riches, honor,

verses.explainer: A proverb about wealth and poverty. Proverbs is realistic about money: it acknowledges its benefits while warning against trusting it, hoarding it, or gaining it dishonestly.

verses.text:

verses.explainer: A proverb about wealth and poverty. Proverbs is realistic about money: it acknowledges its benefits while warning against trusting it, hoarding it, or gaining it dishonestly.

verses.text:

verses.explainer: A proverb about justice. Proverbs insists that the legal system should protect the vulnerable, punish the guilty, and never be corrupted by bribes or favoritism.

verses.text:
that I may give wealth to those who love me.
I fill their treasuries.
verses.explainer: A proverb about wealth and poverty. Proverbs is realistic about money: it acknowledges its benefits while warning against trusting it, hoarding it, or gaining it dishonestly.

verses.explainer: Wisdom declares she existed before creation itself -- 'the LORD possessed me at the beginning of His way.' This became a crucial text in early Christian theology about the pre-existence of Christ.

verses.text:
I was set up from everlasting, from the beginning,

verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.explainer: A proverb drawing on agricultural imagery. In an agrarian society, farming metaphors communicated wisdom about patience, timing, hard work, and the natural consequences of action.

verses.text:
When he established the heavens, I was there;
when he set a circle on the surface of the deep,

verses.text:
when he established the clouds above,
when the springs of the deep became strong,

A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

then I was the craftsman by his side.
I was a delight day by day,
always rejoicing before him,
verses.explainer: Wisdom was beside God as a 'master craftsman' during creation, delighting in the work and rejoicing in humanity. This images presents creation as a joyful, artistic collaboration.

verses.text:
rejoicing in his whole world.
My delight was with the sons of men.
verses.explainer: A proverb about family relationships and parental instruction. Proverbs sees the family as the primary school of wisdom, where character is formed through teaching, discipline, and example.

verses.text:
“Now therefore, my sons, listen to me,

verses.explainer: A proverb about family relationships and parental instruction. Proverbs sees the family as the primary school of wisdom, where character is formed through teaching, discipline, and example.

verses.text:

verses.explainer: A proverb about discipline and correction. Accepting reproof is a hallmark of wisdom; rejecting it is a hallmark of folly. Growth requires the humility to be corrected.

verses.text:
Blessed is the man who hears me,
watching daily at my gates,

verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:

verses.explainer: A proverb grounding practical wisdom in theology -- connecting daily behavior to God's character, will, or sovereign action. In Proverbs, the spiritual and the practical are never separated.

verses.text:
But he who sins against me wrongs his own soul.
All those who hate me love death.”
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

explainer.summary: Two women extend invitations: Lady Wisdom has built her house with seven pillars and prepared a feast -- 'Come, eat my bread and drink my wine.' Lady Folly sits at her door offering stolen water and secret bread. Wisdom's feast leads to life; Folly's feast leads to death and Sheol.
themes: The fear of the LORD restated
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 9 concludes the extended introduction (chs. 1-9) with a dramatic choice: two houses, two feasts, two outcomes. The 'fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom' reappears (9:10), bracketing the entire introduction with this theme.

wordStudies.meaning: Seven columns supporting Wisdom's house. Seven represents completeness -- Wisdom's house is perfectly built and fully established.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Water taken secretly, representing illicit pleasur
wordStudies.meaning: Water taken secretly, representing illicit pleasures. Folly's appeal is the thrill of the forbidden -- what is stolen seems sweeter than what is legitimately obtained.

verses.text:
Wisdom has built her house.
She has carved out her seven pillars.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:

verses.text:
“Whoever is simple, let him turn in here!”

verses.text:

Drink some of the wine which I have mixed!

verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
The fear of Yahweh is the beginning of wisdom.
The knowledge of the Holy One is understanding.
verses.explainer: The restatement of 1:7 at the close of the introduction: the fear of the LORD is not just the beginning of knowledge but the beginning of wisdom itself. Holy living and deep thinking are inseparable.

verses.text:

The years of your life will be increased.
verses.explainer: A proverb about honesty and deception. Integrity in speech and action is foundational to the wisdom life -- God detests dishonesty in all its forms.

verses.text:

verses.text:

verses.text:
To call to those who pass by,

as for him who is void of understanding, she says to him,

But he doesn’t know that the departed spirits are there,

explainer.summary: The first collection of individual Solomonic proverbs begins. Contrasting couplets compare the righteous and the wicked, the wise and the foolish, the diligent and the lazy. Topics include speech, wealth, integrity, and the consequences of character.
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 10 marks a major structural shift: from extended speeches to individual two-line proverbs. Most are antithetical parallelism -- the first line states a truth, the second states its opposite.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A child who has internalized wisdom
wordStudies.meaning: A child who has internalized wisdom. The wise child brings joy; the foolish child brings grief. Character is revealed by its effect on others.
A spring of living water. The righteous person's speech is life-giving, refreshing others like a freshwater spring in an arid land.

verses.explainer: A contrast between wisdom and folly -- the wise and foolish produce different outcomes. The comparison teaches by showing both the positive path and its opposite.

verses.text:
Treasures of wickedness profit nothing,
but righteousness delivers from death.
verses.explainer: A contrast between the righteous and the wicked, showing that character determines outcomes. While exceptions exist (as Ecclesiastes and Job explore), the general pattern holds: righteousness leads to life, wickedness to ruin.

verses.text:
Yahweh will not allow the soul of the righteous to go hungry,
but he thrusts away the desire of the wicked.
verses.explainer: A contrast between the righteous and the wicked, showing that character determines outcomes. While exceptions exist (as Ecclesiastes and Job explore), the general pattern holds: righteousness leads to life, wickedness to ruin.

verses.text:
He becomes poor who works with a lazy hand,
but the hand of the diligent brings wealth.
verses.explainer: A warning against laziness. The sluggard is a recurring character in Proverbs -- always finding excuses, always sleeping, and always surprised when poverty arrives.

verses.text:
He who gathers in summer is a wise son,
but he who sleeps during the harvest is a son who causes shame.
verses.explainer: A proverb about family relationships and parental instruction. Proverbs sees the family as the primary school of wisdom, where character is formed through teaching, discipline, and example.

verses.text:

verses.explainer: A contrast between the righteous and the wicked, showing that character determines outcomes. While exceptions exist (as Ecclesiastes and Job explore), the general pattern holds: righteousness leads to life, wickedness to ruin.

verses.text:

verses.explainer: A contrast between the righteous and the wicked, showing that character determines outcomes. While exceptions exist (as Ecclesiastes and Job explore), the general pattern holds: righteousness leads to life, wickedness to ruin.

verses.text:

verses.text:

verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.explainer: An observation about fools -- those who reject wisdom and pursue destructive patterns. Proverbs uses 'fool' not for intellectual deficiency but for moral and spiritual stubbornness.

verses.text:

verses.text:
He who justifies the wicked, and he who condemns the righteous,

verses.explainer: A contrast between the righteous and the wicked, showing that character determines outcomes. While exceptions exist (as Ecclesiastes and Job explore), the general pattern holds: righteousness leads to life, wickedness to ruin.

verses.text:

verses.explainer: A contrast between wisdom and folly -- the wise and foolish produce different outcomes. The comparison teaches by showing both the positive path and its opposite.

verses.text:
A friend loves at all times;
and a brother is born for adversity.
verses.explainer: The definition of true friendship: love that is not conditional on circumstances. A real friend loves in prosperity and adversity alike. A brother exists precisely for times of trouble.

verses.text:

verses.explainer: A proverb about social relationships. Proverbs teaches that the company you keep shapes who you become -- choose companions wisely.

verses.text:

explainer.summary: An unfriendly person pursues selfish ends. A fool takes no pleasure in understanding. The tongue has the power of life and death. The name of the LORD is a strong tower. A man of many companions may be ruined, but there is a friend who sticks closer than a brother.
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 18 contains several of the book's most memorable sayings: the 'strong tower' of God's name (18:10), the life-and-death power of the tongue (18:21), and the friend closer than a brother (18:24).

wordStudies.meaning: A fortified tower of strength. God's name (His character, reputation, revealed nature) is a fortress where the righteous find safety.

verses.explainer: The contrast between many shallow companions (who can lead to ruin) and one true friend (who sticks closer than family). Quality of friendship matters infinitely more than quantity.

explainer.summary: Better to be poor and walk in integrity than to be a fool with perverse lips. Wealth adds many friends; poverty drives them away. Whoever is kind to the poor lends to the LORD. Discipline your child while there is hope.
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 19 addresses poverty, wealth, and their social effects with remarkable honesty. It acknowledges that wealth attracts friends (not always sincere) while poverty repels them -- without endorsing this pattern.

wordStudies.meaning: Giving to the poor is literally lending to God, who will repay the investment. This astonishing metaphor makes generosity a divine transaction.

explainer.summary: Wine is a mocker, strong drink a brawler. It is honorable to avoid strife. Plans succeed with many advisors. The spirit of a person is the LORD's lamp, searching the inner depths. Differing weights and measures are detestable to the LORD.
themes: God's searching of the human spirit
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 20 balances warnings (about alcohol, strife, dishonesty) with observations about God's intimate knowledge of the human interior. The LORD's lamp searching inner depths (20:27) is a striking image.

wordStudies.meaning: God's lamp is the human spirit -- God searches the innermost parts of a person through their own conscience. The human spirit is God's instrument for self-examination.

explainer.summary: The king's heart is in the LORD's hand like a stream of water. Every way seems right to a person, but the LORD weighs the heart. To do righteousness and justice is more acceptable than sacrifice. The horse is prepared for battle, but victory comes from the LORD.
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 21 emphasizes divine sovereignty -- God directs kings, weighs hearts, and determines battle outcomes. Human planning is important but ultimately subordinate to God's will.

wordStudies.meaning: God evaluates the inner motivations, not just outward actions. A person may justify themselves, but God examines what truly drives them.

explainer.summary: A good name is more desirable than great riches. The rich and poor have this in common: the LORD is the Maker of them all. Train up a child in the way they should go. Do not exploit the poor or crush the afflicted. The 'Thirty Sayings of the Wise' begin at verse 17.

explainer.summary: Do not envy the wicked. By wisdom a house is built. If you falter in times of trouble, how small is your strength! Rescue those being led to slaughter. The field of the sluggard is overgrown with thorns. A little sleep, a little slumber -- poverty arrives like an armed man.

explainer.summary: Hezekiah's scribes compile more of Solomon's proverbs. A word fitly spoken is like apples of gold in settings of silver. Heap burning coals on your enemy's head by treating them kindly. Like cold water to a weary soul is good news from a distant land.
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 25 begins the second major collection of Solomonic proverbs (25-29), compiled by Hezekiah's scribes about 250 years after Solomon. These proverbs use more extended metaphors and comparisons.

wordStudies.translatedAs: At its proper time and in its proper way

wordStudies.translatedAs: Kindness to enemies produces burning shame (convic

explainer.summary: Extended observations about fools, sluggards, and gossips. Honor is not fitting for a fool. As a dog returns to its vomit, so a fool repeats his folly. The sluggard imagines a lion in the road. A gossip's words are like choice morsels.
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 26 clusters proverbs by topic: fools (1-12), sluggards (13-16), and troublemakers (17-28). The famous 'dog returns to its vomit' proverb (26:11) appears here.

wordStudies.meaning: What has been expelled. A dog eating what it ejected is revolting but natural for dogs. A fool repeating expelled folly is equally revolting but equally predictable.

explainer.summary: Do not boast about tomorrow. Let someone else praise you. Anger is cruel and fury overwhelming, but who can stand before jealousy? Open rebuke is better than hidden love. Faithful are the wounds of a friend. Iron sharpens iron.
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 27 is rich in friendship proverbs. 'Iron sharpens iron' (27:17) and 'faithful are the wounds of a friend' (27:6) present a robust vision of friendship that includes honest challenge, not just comfort.

wordStudies.meaning: To sharpen, to make keen. Iron against iron creates friction that hones both blades. Genuine friendship involves the productive friction of honest challenge.

thenVsNow.then: Comfortable friendships that avoid all disagreement do not produce growth. The best relationships include honest challenge -- the 'iron' of accountability that sharpens both parties.

explainer.summary: The words of Agur son of Jakeh. He confesses his own ignorance and asks profound questions about God. He prays for neither poverty nor riches. Numerical proverbs observe wonders: four things that are never satisfied, four things too wonderful to understand, four things that are small but exceedingly wise.
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 30 stands apart from the Solomonic collections. Agur's confession of ignorance and his prayer for contentment (neither poverty nor riches) offer a refreshingly humble voice after Solomon's confident wisdom.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A burden, a weighty utterance, a prophetic oracle
wordStudies.meaning: A burden, a weighty utterance, a prophetic oracle. Agur's words carry prophetic weight despite his confession of personal ignorance.

explainer.summary: King Lemuel's mother teaches him about sobriety, justice for the poor, and the qualities of an excellent wife. The famous acrostic poem (31:10-31) describes the 'woman of valor' -- an entrepreneurial, generous, wise, and strong woman whose worth exceeds rubies.
themes: A mother's counsel to a king
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 31 bookends the book perfectly. Proverbs opened with a father teaching his son; it closes with a mother teaching her son. The 'woman of valor' poem is an acrostic (each verse begins with a successive Hebrew letter), celebrating wisdom embodied in a woman's life.

wordStudies.meaning: Strength, capability, valor, wealth. The same word used for mighty warriors. This woman is not passive or decorative -- she is a force of nature, combining strength with character.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Precious stones, possibly pearls or coral
wordStudies.meaning: Precious stones, possibly pearls or coral. Her value exceeds the most precious commodities in the ancient world -- she cannot be purchased at any price.

verses.explainer: The opening of the famous acrostic poem: 'Who can find a woman of valor? Her worth is far above rubies.' The Hebrew 'chayil' (valor, strength) is the same word used for mighty warriors -- this is not a delicate, passive woman.

thenVsNow.then: The 'woman of valor' defies both ancient and modern stereotypes. She manages businesses, makes investment decisions, cares for the poor, speaks wisdom, and does it all with strength and dignity.

verses.text:
The heart of her husband trusts in her.
He shall have no lack of gain.
verses.explainer: A proverb about marriage and domestic life. Proverbs recognizes that the quality of one's closest relationship profoundly shapes the quality of one's entire life.

verses.text:
She does him good, and not harm,
all the days of her life.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
She seeks wool and flax,
and works eagerly with her hands.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
She is like the merchant ships.
She brings her bread from afar.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
She rises also while it is yet night,
gives food to her household,
and portions for her servant girls.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
She considers a field, and buys it.
With the fruit of her hands, she plants a vineyard.
verses.explainer: A proverb about planning and counsel. Wise decisions require input from multiple advisors -- no one person sees every angle of a situation.

verses.text:
She arms her waist with strength,
and makes her arms strong.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
She perceives that her merchandise is profitable.
Her lamp doesn’t go out by night.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
She lays her hands to the distaff,
and her hands hold the spindle.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
She opens her arms to the poor;
yes, she extends her hands to the needy.
verses.explainer: A proverb about wealth and poverty. Proverbs is realistic about money: it acknowledges its benefits while warning against trusting it, hoarding it, or gaining it dishonestly.

verses.text:
She is not afraid of the snow for her household;
for all her household are clothed with scarlet.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
She makes for herself carpets of tapestry.
Her clothing is fine linen and purple.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
Her husband is respected in the gates,
when he sits among the elders of the land.
verses.explainer: A proverb about marriage and domestic life. Proverbs recognizes that the quality of one's closest relationship profoundly shapes the quality of one's entire life.

verses.text:
She makes linen garments and sells them,
and delivers sashes to the merchant.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
Strength and dignity are her clothing.
She laughs at the time to come.
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
She opens her mouth with wisdom.
Faithful instruction is on her tongue.
verses.explainer: A proverb about speech -- one of Proverbs' most frequent topics. Words have real power to build up or tear down, and controlling the tongue is presented as one of wisdom's hardest and most important disciplines.

verses.text:
She looks well to the ways of her household,
and doesn’t eat the bread of idleness.
verses.explainer: A warning against laziness. The sluggard is a recurring character in Proverbs -- always finding excuses, always sleeping, and always surprised when poverty arrives.

verses.text:
Her children rise up and call her blessed.
Her husband also praises her:
verses.explainer: A proverb about marriage and domestic life. Proverbs recognizes that the quality of one's closest relationship profoundly shapes the quality of one's entire life.

verses.text:
“Many women do noble things,
but you excel them all.”
verses.explainer: A wisdom observation about practical living. Proverbs teaches through concise, memorable sayings that capture general truths about how life works under God's moral order.

verses.text:
Charm is deceitful, and beauty is vain;
but a woman who fears Yahweh, she shall be praised.
verses.explainer: The poem's climactic thesis: charm deceives and beauty fades, but a woman who fears the LORD is worthy of praise. Inner character, not external appearance, determines lasting worth.

thenVsNow.then: In a culture obsessed with physical appearance, this verse insists that character -- specifically, a God-centered character -- is the only beauty that lasts. Charm and beauty are not bad, just insufficient.

verses.text:
Give her of the fruit of her hands!
Let her works praise her in the gates!
